title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Union support for STREAM raises stakes on jobs versus power and water strains in mid-Michigan

lines.text: Good morning and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's Saturday, July 11, and we're diving into fresh developments as the sun comes up on another busy week for data centers across the state.
The big story today is that the carpenters union has thrown its support behind the STREAM data center project.

Exactly. That endorsement signals hundreds of construction jobs if the project gets approved, but it also highlights the trade-offs communities in mid-Michigan are facing on roads, water, and electricity.
Graham, why does union backing matter so much here? Is this a new wrinkle in these fights?
It's significant because it brings organized labor into the conversation at a time when several townships are already pausing projects to study the impacts.
Right, like Haring Township in Wexford County voting today on a six-month moratorium to block new applications while they write permanent rules.
Residents there are worried about sudden spikes in power demand and noise from these hyperscale facilities, so the pause lets officials dig into water use and tax effects first.
And how does the STREAM endorsement fit with that bigger picture of local pushback?
It raises the stakes because the union sees steady paychecks for local workers, but nearby towns still have to weigh those gains against new demands on infrastructure.
We've seen this tension before with other proposals. What kind of history are we looking at for these projects in Michigan?
Data centers have been eyeing sites across the state for a while now, with debates popping up in places like Ingham and Washtenaw counties over noise, power strain, and lost farmland.
And the power side is huge. A local analyst in Monroe warned yesterday that unchecked growth will mean new plants and upgrades, with costs flowing to residential ratepayers through the utilities.
That's the core worry for many households — higher electric bills without enough oversight on how much these facilities will draw.
Water is another big one. In Saline, construction at one site has already raised questions about dewatering and impacts on nearby ponds and groundwater.
Officials there reported a resident pond dropping about two feet while groundwater levels fell four to five feet, and the township is monitoring through the summer.
So for a place like STREAM, if approved, what does that mean for the surrounding area in practical terms?
Hundreds of construction jobs in the short term, plus potential long-term property tax revenue, but also added pressure on local roads, water supplies, and the grid.
And we've got ratepayers rallying next week in Kent County and beyond to highlight exactly those concerns about AI facilities draining resources.
More than 50 communities are involved in that July 18 event, showing the debate is moving beyond town halls into the streets.
Meanwhile in Mason, residents are accusing outsiders of faking grassroots support in their local fight. How does that kind of friction affect decision-making?
It fractures trust right when officials are weighing next steps on noise, power, and farmland issues in Ingham County.
On the political side, all four GOP governor candidates backed tax breaks for data centers during their Grand Rapids debate yesterday.
They pointed to construction jobs and revenue for rural townships, though voters didn't hear much detail on household electric rates or water supplies.
And yesterday Rep. Debbie Dingell released letters probing a University of Michigan data center plan with Los Alamos ties, flagging power and water worries.
That exchange keeps the spotlight on university involvement and what it could mean for residents near potential sites.
Also yesterday, Meta's big Wyoming project in Kent County cleared early hurdles — a 715,000-square-foot campus that will pull substantial electricity.
Neighbors there are already asking what the power draw means for their bills as the facility reshapes local tax rolls and grid planning.
So pulling it together, Graham, the union support for STREAM adds momentum on the jobs front, but the moratorium in Haring and the rallies show communities are hitting pause to study the downsides.
Right, and what happens next depends on those local votes and whether projects can address the infrastructure concerns before moving forward.
Let's shift to some quick hits from the last day or so.
Haring Township supervisors are set to vote today on that six-month moratorium to halt new data center proposals while they draft permanent rules.
Residents there are focused on power spikes and noise, giving officials time to study water and tax impacts first.
Yesterday in Mason, accusations flew that fake grassroots support is being manufactured in the data center debate.
Ingham County neighbors are worried about noise, power strain, and lost farmland as officials weigh the next steps.
Also yesterday, all four Republican candidates for governor voiced support for tax incentives during their Grand Rapids debate.
They highlighted jobs and revenue for rural areas, but details on electric rates and water were light.
A Monroe utility analyst warned yesterday that data-center growth will require new power plants and upgrades, costs that hit ratepayers through DTE and Consumers filings.
That comes as several large projects look at southeast Michigan sites.
Ratepayers are rallying July 18 across more than 50 communities, with Kent County activists leading the charge on power and water demands from AI facilities.
It shows the debate is reaching the streets now.
Meta's Wyoming campus cleared early city hurdles yesterday — a massive 715,000-square-foot facility in Kent County.
The power draw is already prompting questions from neighbors about their bills.
And in Saline, construction digging has threatened ponds and groundwater, with a resident pond reportedly dropping two feet.
The township is continuing summer monitoring after the contractor added water without prior notice.
Rep. Debbie Dingell released letters yesterday probing the U-M data center plan with Los Alamos ties.
The exchange spotlights local concerns over power, water, and university involvement.
Now let's look ahead to what's on deck for listeners who want to weigh in.
Monday, July 13, the Lansing City Council Development & Planning committee has a public hearing on a proposed 182-day data center moratorium.
That's a chance for residents to speak directly on the pause.
Then on Tuesday, July 14, the Saline Township Board of Trustees will vote on a 12-year, 50% tax abatement for Oracle America Cloud Services tied to the Related Digital AI data center project.
There's also a public hearing that day on the Industrial Facilities Exemption Certificate.
Those are real opportunities for folks in the area to show up and share their thoughts on the incentives and the project.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Carpenters Union Backs STREAM Project as Townships Weigh Moratoriums
